ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper does not introduce a novel generative model—its LSTM approach and n-gram baseline closely mirror prior work such as GhostWriter (2015)—but it makes a notable, well-differentiated contribution in evaluation: a comprehensive, multi-faceted methodology for rap-lyric ghostwriting, a manually annotated corpus for style matching across 13 artists, and a fully automated improvement of a prior semi-automatic metric. These evaluation-focused advances are a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods and would inform experts seeking reproducible, systematic comparisons in creative generation, even though the core modeling is incremental. | B(4): The paper’s primary novelty lies in its comprehensive and empirically grounded evaluation methodology (manual fluency/coherence/style matching plus automated rhyme-density and similarity regression) and the release of a 13-artist corpus annotated for style — contributions that go beyond prior generation work such as GhostWriter (2015), which focused on LSTM-based lyric generation. Automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) and showing it replicates prior results is a useful practical advance, but the generative technique (LSTM vs. n-gram) is not itself new. Overall, the work meaningfully differentiates itself by advancing evaluation resources and metrics for rap ghostwriting, representing notable but not radical novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not introduce a novel generative model—its LSTM approach and n-gram baseline closely mirror prior work such as GhostWriter (2015)—but it makes a notable, well-differentiated contribution in evaluation: a comprehensive, multi-faceted methodology for rap-lyric ghostwriting, a manually annotated corpus for style matching across 13 artists, and a fully automated improvement of a prior semi-automatic metric. These evaluation-focused advances are a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods and would inform experts seeking reproducible, systematic comparisons in creative generation, even though the core modeling is incremental.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s primary novelty lies in its comprehensive and empirically grounded evaluation methodology (manual fluency/coherence/style matching plus automated rhyme-density and similarity regression) and the release of a 13-artist corpus annotated for style — contributions that go beyond prior generation work such as GhostWriter (2015), which focused on LSTM-based lyric generation. Automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) and showing it replicates prior results is a useful practical advance, but the generative technique (LSTM vs. n-gram) is not itself new. Overall, the work meaningfully differentiates itself by advancing evaluation resources and metrics for rap ghostwriting, representing notable but not radical novelty.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear structure (introduction → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions (evaluation methodology, automated improvement, and annotated corpus) early on. Notation and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples tied to metrics like rhyme density and Match% appear informative for reproducing the evaluation. Writing quality is good with explicit limitations discussed, though the summary leaves ambiguity about code availability and some preprocessing heuristics that could be more precisely documented for full reproducibility. From a presentation standpoint this is a solid paper with only minor clarity gaps to address. | B(4): From the supplied summary, the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: a comprehensive evaluation methodology, an automated improvement to prior evaluation, and a curated corpus for 13 artists. Notation and metrics appear consistent and well defined, with hyperparameters and related work adequately reported; figures/tables and examples (rhyme density, Match% metrics, line-level annotations) seem informative. Writing quality is generally good, though the authors acknowledge heuristic preprocessing and dataset noise which suggests a few sections could benefit from clearer treatment of limitations and artifact effects. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarity issues.

From a presentation standpoint this is a solid paper with only minor clarity gaps to address.

Notation and metrics appear consistent and well defined, with hyperparameters and related work adequately reported; figures/tables and examples (rhyme density, Match% metrics, line-level annotations) seem informative. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper addresses a concrete and useful problem—evaluation of creative language generation—by proposing a comprehensive manual+automated methodology and an automated refinement of a prior metric, and by releasing a 13-artist annotated lyrics corpus that enables reproducible stylistic evaluation. The contributions are practically valuable (a standard evaluation protocol, a dataset, and metric improvements) and are likely to be adopted by researchers working on stylistic or creative generation, but they are fairly specific to lyric-style tasks and do not fundamentally change modeling approaches; absence of mentioned code release and some dataset preprocessing caveats limit immediate broad uptake. Overall, the work is a solid, moderately impactful contribution that enables better comparative evaluation but is not transformative for the field at large. | B(3): This work provides a useful, practical evaluation methodology and a curated, annotated corpus for a concrete creative language task (rap ghostwriting), which will be valuable for researchers studying stylized generation and for reproducing/benchmarking models. The fully automated improvement to repetition-penalized evaluation and the combined manual+automated protocol strengthen measurement practices, but the contribution is fairly domain-specific (13 artists, rap lyrics) and constrained by known automatic-evaluation limits and a single LSTM baseline. Consequently it is a solid, reusable contribution that will influence follow-up empirical work but is unlikely to radically shift how the field conceptualizes creative language generation.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a concrete and useful problem—evaluation of creative language generation—by proposing a comprehensive manual+automated methodology and an automated refinement of a prior metric, and by releasing a 13-artist annotated lyrics corpus that enables reproducible stylistic evaluation. The contributions are practically valuable (a standard evaluation protocol, a dataset, and metric improvements) and are likely to be adopted by researchers working on stylistic or creative generation, but they are fairly specific to lyric-style tasks and do not fundamentally change modeling approaches; absence of mentioned code release and some dataset preprocessing caveats limit immediate broad uptake. Overall, the work is a solid, moderately impactful contribution that enables better comparative evaluation but is not transformative for the field at large.

reviewer_b.justification: This work provides a useful, practical evaluation methodology and a curated, annotated corpus for a concrete creative language task (rap ghostwriting), which will be valuable for researchers studying stylized generation and for reproducing/benchmarking models. The fully automated improvement to repetition-penalized evaluation and the combined manual+automated protocol strengthen measurement practices, but the contribution is fairly domain-specific (13 artists, rap lyrics) and constrained by known automatic-evaluation limits and a single LSTM baseline. Consequently it is a solid, reusable contribution that will influence follow-up empirical work but is unlikely to radically shift how the field conceptualizes creative language generation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a broad, multi-pronged evaluation (automated rhyme/similarity measures plus detailed manual annotations for fluency, coherence, and style matching) and supplies a 13-artist corpus and reported hyperparameters, which together substantially support its claims. However, experimental comparisons are relatively narrow (only an n-gram baseline versus an LSTM) and the summary does not describe systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses to training/regression choices; code/data availability is unclear which impedes exact reproduction despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work coverage appears adequate (e.g., builds on prior LSTM rap generation work) and limitations are candidly discussed, so the paper is strong but missing a few reproducibility and robustness checks that would justify an “extremely thorough” rating. | B(4): The paper provides a substantial experimental evaluation combining manual annotations and automated metrics across a novel 13-artist corpus, compares LSTM against an n-gram baseline, reports hyperparameters, and explicitly discusses limitations (e.g., preprocessing heuristics, inability to model new vocabulary). However, I see no mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses to isolate model components or training dynamics, and code availability is unclear which weakens reproducibility despite a public corpus. Related work is covered adequately (e.g., prior LSTM rap generation), and failure cases/negative results are discussed, supporting a solid but not exhaustive empirical treatment.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a broad, multi-pronged evaluation (automated rhyme/similarity measures plus detailed manual annotations for fluency, coherence, and style matching) and supplies a 13-artist corpus and reported hyperparameters, which together substantially support its claims. However, experimental comparisons are relatively narrow (only an n-gram baseline versus an LSTM) and the summary does not describe systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses to training/regression choices; code/data availability is unclear which impedes exact reproduction despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work coverage appears adequate (e.g., builds on prior LSTM rap generation work) and limitations are candidly discussed, so the paper is strong but missing a few reproducibility and robustness checks that would justify an “extremely thorough” rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a substantial experimental evaluation combining manual annotations and automated metrics across a novel 13-artist corpus, compares LSTM against an n-gram baseline, reports hyperparameters, and explicitly discusses limitations (e.g., preprocessing heuristics, inability to model new vocabulary). However, I see no mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses to isolate model components or training dynamics, and code availability is unclear which weakens reproducibility despite a public corpus. Related work is covered adequately (e.g., prior LSTM rap generation), and failure cases/negative results are discussed, supporting a solid but not exhaustive empirical treatment.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates an LSTM ghostwriter against an n-gram baseline and explicitly reproduces and automates prior evaluation (Potash et al., 2015), which are sensible but minimal baselines for this task. Comparisons appear fair (same corpus, metrics, and per-artist training), and the authors acknowledge limitations and when prior measures reproduce earlier results. However, the experimental comparison is incomplete: it lacks stronger contemporaneous baselines (e.g., other neural or conditioned generation approaches such as prior LSTM-based GhostWriter work) and more diverse baselines for style modeling, reducing confidence that reported gains are well contextualized against the state of the art. | B(4): The paper performs good, relevant comparisons: it implements an LSTM generative model and contrasts it with an n-gram baseline, and it explicitly improves and automates the evaluation from Potash et al. (2015), reproducing prior results. Comparison conditions are reasonably fair (same lyrics corpus, reported hyperparameters, shared evaluation metrics like rhyme density and similarity), and the paper acknowledges limitations and when automated measures fall short. However, it omits direct empirical comparison to other contemporary neural baselines (e.g., prior LSTM-based GhostWriter efforts) beyond reproducing evaluation behaviour, so while the survey and analysis are solid, the empirical baseline set is not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates an LSTM ghostwriter against an n-gram baseline and explicitly reproduces and automates prior evaluation (Potash et al., 2015), which are sensible but minimal baselines for this task. Comparisons appear fair (same corpus, metrics, and per-artist training), and the authors acknowledge limitations and when prior measures reproduce earlier results. However, the experimental comparison is incomplete: it lacks stronger contemporaneous baselines (e.g., other neural or conditioned generation approaches such as prior LSTM-based GhostWriter work) and more diverse baselines for style modeling, reducing confidence that reported gains are well contextualized against the state of the art.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper performs good, relevant comparisons: it implements an LSTM generative model and contrasts it with an n-gram baseline, and it explicitly improves and automates the evaluation from Potash et al. (2015), reproducing prior results. Comparison conditions are reasonably fair (same lyrics corpus, reported hyperparameters, shared evaluation metrics like rhyme density and similarity), and the paper acknowledges limitations and when automated measures fall short. However, it omits direct empirical comparison to other contemporary neural baselines (e.g., prior LSTM-based GhostWriter efforts) beyond reproducing evaluation behaviour, so while the survey and analysis are solid, the empirical baseline set is not fully comprehensive.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits squarely in the NLP venue’s remit: it proposes a comprehensive evaluation methodology for creative language generation, releases an annotated corpus of 13 artists, and offers an automated improvement to a prior metric while evaluating an LSTM and n-gram baselines. From a reproducibility-methodologist perspective the experimental design and mixed manual/automated evaluations are appropriate and relevant, and hyperparameters are reported, but missing details (code not mentioned), heuristic preprocessing, and possible dataset contamination warrant caution. Overall the contribution is broadly suitable for a top conference, albeit with minor gaps that should be addressed to fully meet the community’s reproducibility standards. | B(4): This paper sits largely within the scope of top NLP venues: it tackles creative language generation, offers a new evaluation methodology combining manual and automated measures, and releases a 13-artist corpus useful to the community. The work uses expected methods (LSTM baseline, n-gram comparison, rhyme-density and similarity metrics) and reports hyperparameters and limitations transparently, making it relevant to researchers in generation and evaluation. My only minor concern is a degree of domain specificity (rap lyrics) which may limit immediate generalization, but the evaluation contributions and dataset still address a broad audience interested in stylistic generation and metrics.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely in the NLP venue’s remit: it proposes a comprehensive evaluation methodology for creative language generation, releases an annotated corpus of 13 artists, and offers an automated improvement to a prior metric while evaluating an LSTM and n-gram baselines. From a reproducibility-methodologist perspective the experimental design and mixed manual/automated evaluations are appropriate and relevant, and hyperparameters are reported, but missing details (code not mentioned), heuristic preprocessing, and possible dataset contamination warrant caution. Overall the contribution is broadly suitable for a top conference, albeit with minor gaps that should be addressed to fully meet the community’s reproducibility standards.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits largely within the scope of top NLP venues: it tackles creative language generation, offers a new evaluation methodology combining manual and automated measures, and releases a 13-artist corpus useful to the community. The work uses expected methods (LSTM baseline, n-gram comparison, rhyme-density and similarity metrics) and reports hyperparameters and limitations transparently, making it relevant to researchers in generation and evaluation. My only minor concern is a degree of domain specificity (rap lyrics) which may limit immediate generalization, but the evaluation contributions and dataset still address a broad audience interested in stylistic generation and metrics.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are generally clear and supported by experiments combining manual annotations and automated metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, Match% etc.) applied to a 13-artist corpus and LSTM versus n-gram baselines. However, reproducibility is weakened by lack of explicit code release, heuristic preprocessing and potential dataset contamination, and the evaluation could benefit from stronger baselines and clearer statistical analyses (confidence intervals/significance tests) to support claims about improvements. Mathematical content appears not central and no errors were indicated in the summary. Overall the methodology is sound but has minor gaps that should be addressed to fully substantiate the conclusions. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported by experiments: it combines manual and automated evaluations, introduces a fully automated repetition-penalized rhyme-density measure that replicates prior results, and releases a 13-artist annotated corpus used to evaluate an LSTM versus an n-gram baseline. Technical methodology is mostly sound, but there are minor concerns: preprocessing is heuristic and the corpus may contain non-verse text, only a single (n-gram) baseline is reported, and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing is given. These limitations are acknowledged in the paper and do not appear to undermine the primary experimental findings, hence a near-flawless technical rating with minor issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are generally clear and supported by experiments combining manual annotations and automated metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, Match% etc.) applied to a 13-artist corpus and LSTM versus n-gram baselines. However, reproducibility is weakened by lack of explicit code release, heuristic preprocessing and potential dataset contamination, and the evaluation could benefit from stronger baselines and clearer statistical analyses (confidence intervals/significance tests) to support claims about improvements. Mathematical content appears not central and no errors were indicated in the summary. Overall the methodology is sound but has minor gaps that should be addressed to fully substantiate the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported by experiments: it combines manual and automated evaluations, introduces a fully automated repetition-penalized rhyme-density measure that replicates prior results, and releases a 13-artist annotated corpus used to evaluate an LSTM versus an n-gram baseline. Technical methodology is mostly sound, but there are minor concerns: preprocessing is heuristic and the corpus may contain non-verse text, only a single (n-gram) baseline is reported, and no explicit mention of statistical significance testing is given. These limitations are acknowledged in the paper and do not appear to undermine the primary experimental findings, hence a near-flawless technical rating with minor issues.

main_claims: Presented a comprehensive evaluation methodology for rap lyric ghostwriting combining manual (fluency, coherence, style matching) and automated evaluations.
Introduced a fully automated improvement to a prior semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) that penalizes repetitive text and replicates prior results.
Provided a publicly available corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists annotated for stylistic similarity and used it to evaluate an LSTM-based ghostwriter.
_summary.methodology: Use an LSTM RNN (with per-artist models trained for many iterations) as the generative system and compare it to an n-gram baseline; evaluate generated verses via automated metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity regression) and manual annotations for fluency, coherence, and style matching.
datasets: Corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists (collected from The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive - OHHLA.com)
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot assess vocabulary, tone, and themes preferred by an artist.
Preprocessing was heuristic; dataset may still contain non-verse text (dialogue or chorus).
LSTM model has inability to integrate new vocabulary, making desirable similarity scores difficult to achieve.
Rhyme density regression can hit average rhyme density at negative iterations early in training (artefact noted).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP focused on experimental design and reproducible evaluations for generative models; several years experience implementing, benchmarking, and deploying sequence models.
traits: insists on clear descriptions of model architectures, training regimes, hyperparameters, and compute budgets to enable replication
values strong baselines, ablation studies, and statistically meaningful comparisons (confidence intervals, significance tests)
checks evaluation methodology closely (metric validity, calibration, annotator procedures, inter-annotator agreement) and sensitivity analyses
looks for publicly available code/data, concrete instructions to reproduce results, and measures taken to avoid leakage or overfitting
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focused on empirical rigor and reproducibility: evaluates whether experiments and evaluations are designed and reported so claims are supported and results can be reliably reproduced.

reviewer_b.name: Creative Language & Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP and computational creativity studying stylized language generation, authorial style transfer, and the societal implications of creative AI; extensive publication record on datasets and evaluation for creative text.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work on creative language generation and style imitation, and whether the contribution advances the field
values clearly articulated research questions, dataset curation quality, and the utility of released resources for downstream research
attends to broader impact, ethical considerations (artist rights, cultural sensitivity), and realistic claims about capabilities and limitations
notices whether evaluation captures meaningful aspects of creative quality (originality, style, persona) beyond surface metrics
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper for novelty, dataset and resource value, and broader scientific and societal impact, balancing technical contributions with practical significance.
